figures.caption: Figure 3: Distribution statistics of the SGMRI-VQA dataset. Top row (a–c): training set (32,014 QA pairs). Bottom row (d–f): test set (9,293 QA pairs). (a,d) Distribution of Q&A pairs per sample. (b,e) Question length distribution. (c,f) Response length distribution stratified by answer format— closed-ended, choice, and open-ended—and chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning traces. Normalized frequency is the item count divided by the total count across all items.

Figure 6: Fibula side annotation interface. A purpose-built web tool for annotating fibula laterality across knee MRI volumes. Since the fibula is always lateral to the knee joint, annotating which side of the image the fibula appears on determines the medial/lateral orientation for each volume.

Figure 7: Examples of GPT-4o hallucination errors corrected during quality assurance. Hemisphere laterality spatial errors: GPT-4o assigns incorrect hemisphere labels that contradict its own bounding box coordinates. Knee medial/lateral spatial swap and omitted findings in multi-pathology volumes.

Figure 5: Web-based quality assurance interface. The purpose-built annotation tool used by the physician for reviewing and correcting GPT-4o-generated QA pairs. The interface displays annotated MRI images with bounding box overlays, the generated question-answer pairs with reasoning traces, ground truth annotations, and an animated viewer for frame-by-frame volume inspection.

Figure 1: Examples of the diverse question tasks in SGMRI-VQA. Each task is illustrated with real MRI slices and chain-of-thought reasoning, generated at both volume-level and image-level granularities across brain and knee domains, with tasks providing spatially grounded bounding box coordinates.

Figure 4: Qualitative localization examples from the finetuned Qwen3-VL-8B. (a) Image-level predictions on single MRI slices. (b) Volume-level predictions across multi-frame sequences. Green boxes denote ground-truth annotations with pathology labels; red dashed boxes denote model predictions. For both image-level and volume-level predictions, we show the input image(s), evaluation question, ground-truth answer, and model output.
Table 1: Comparison with medical VQA benchmarks. O = Open-ended, C = Closed (Yes/No), SC = Single-choice, MC = Multiple-choice, CoT = chain-of-thought reasoning.
Table 4: Volume-level task performance on the MRI-VQA test set. All values in %. Best in bold, second best underlined.

Table 3: Image-level task performance on the MRI-VQA test set. Metrics: A-Score (exact-match accuracy) for closed-ended tasks, V-Score (mIoU) for spatial grounding, AR-Score (GPT-4o-mini judge + NLG) for open-ended generation. All values in %. Best in bold, second best underlined.
Figure 2: Finding category distribution. Inner ring: normal vs. abnormal. Outer ring: finding categories proportional to QA pair count.

Figure 8: Overview of the four GPT-4o prompt templates used for QA pair generation. Each card summarizes the role, input format, rules, template variables, five hierarchical tasks, reasoning and expected JSON output schema for one of the four prompt types: brain volume-level, knee volume-level, brain image-level, and knee image-level.
